attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health check. She has no specific complaints. Her medical history includes generalised osteoarthritis, for which she takes paracetamol, and hypertension, managed with perindopril. A maternal aunt was diagnosed with carcinoma of the colon at the age of 65. There is no other family history of bowel cancer. The patient wonders if she needs to take any action regarding bowel cancer screening. She has no digestive tract symptoms and has never been part of any screening program.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding colorectal cancer screening?
options: No further action needs to be taken
attributes.explanation:
This woman is considered low risk for colorectal cancer, based on her family history and age. She does not have any first-degree relatives who have been diagnosed with colorectal cancer, and her age is beyond the recommended screening range. Current recommendations are that routine two-yearly faecal occult blood test screening should be offered to patients aged between 50 and 74 in this risk category.

No further action needs to be taken is the most appropriate advice for this patient. Screening is typically not offered for patients older than 74 as the benefits of early detection are diminished and patients generally become less fit for intervention as they age.

Biennial faecal occult blood testing is not recommended for this patient due to her age. This screening method is typically offered to individuals between 50 and 74 years old who are at average risk for colorectal cancer.

Annual faecal immunochemical testing is another form of stool-based screening, but like faecal occult blood testing, it is not recommended for individuals over 74 years old who are at average risk.

Colonoscopy is an invasive procedure that carries risks, especially in older patients. It is not recommended as a routine screening tool for low-risk individuals over 74 years old.

Virtual colonoscopy, also known as CT colonography, is a less invasive alternative to traditional colonoscopy. However, it is still not recommended as a routine screening method for low-risk individuals over 74 years old.

It's important to note that while the patient has a family history of colorectal cancer in a second-degree relative (maternal aunt), this does not significantly increase her risk. Significant family history typically involves first-degree relatives (parents, siblings, or children) diagnosed with colorectal cancer, especially if diagnosed before age 60. The patient should be advised to report any new digestive symptoms promptly, but routine screening is not indicated at her age.

The current colorectal cancer risk categories and screening recommendations based on family history are as follows:

Category 1 - similar risk as the general population

  • Asymptomatic people with no family history of colorectal cancer OR

  • One 1st-degree relative with CRC ≥60 years

  • Screening: FOBT should be completed every 2 years from 45 years old as per usual screening protocol. Colonoscopy is generally not recommended.

Category 2 - moderately increased risk

  • Asymptomatic people with one 1st-degree relative CRC diagnosed <60 years OR

  • Two 1st-degree relatives with CRC at any age or one 1st-degree and one or more 2nd-degree relatives diagnosed at any age

  • Screening: Colonoscopy should be offered 5 yearly starting at 10 years younger than the earliest diagnosis of CRC in a 1st-degree relative, or age 50 (whichever is earliest).

Category 3 - high risk, excluding hereditary cancer syndromes

  • Asymptomatic with two 1st-degree and one 2nd-degree relative with colorectal cancer, with at least one diagnosed before 50 years old OR

  • Two 1st-degree relatives and two or more 2nd-degree relatives diagnosed with CRC at any age OR

  • Three or more 1st-degree relatives with CRC at any age.

  • Screening: refer to the familial cancer clinic for genetic assessment before a screening decision is made if Lynch syndrome has not been excluded. If Lynch syndrome is excluded, colonoscopy should be offered every 5 years starting at 10 years younger than the earliest age of diagnosis of colorectal cancer in a first-degree relative or age 40 years (whichever is earliest)
learningPoints: Routine colorectal cancer screening for low-risk patients is not indicated after the age of 74.
Family history of colorectal cancer in second-degree relatives does not significantly increase risk.
references: Parkin CJ BS, Mirbagheri N. Colorectal cancer screening in Australia: An update. Aust J Gen Pract. 2018; 47:859-63.

Cancer Council Australia. Clinical practice guidelines for the prevention, early detection and management of colorectal cancer: Risk and screening based on family history. 2024. Available at: https://app.magicapp.org/#/guideline/jz5DdE

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
